1: wayyippōl yôsēp ʿal-pĕnê ʾāḇîw wayyēḇk ʿālāyw wayyiššaq-lô — "Joseph threw himself on his father's face, wept over him, and kissed him." Wayyippōl ʿal-pĕnê (he fell on his face) — the prostration-of-grief over the beloved dead; the same posture used for reverence and overwhelming emotion. The face-contact (ʿal-pĕnê ʾāḇîw) and the kiss (wayyiššaq-lô) — the most intimate forms of physical contact with the dead. The reunion Joseph longed for across twenty-two years (Genesis 45:3; Genesis 46:29-30) has ended; his weeping now mirrors the weeping in the reunion scene.
2: wayĕṣaw yôsēp ʾet-ʿăḇāḏāyw ʾet-hārōpĕʾîm laḥănōṭ ʾet-ʾāḇîw wayyaḥăniṭû hārōpĕʾîm ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — "Joseph directed the physicians in his service to embalm (laḥănōṭ) his father." Ḥānaṭ (H2590, to embalm — likely an Egyptian loanword or technical term; appears only here and v3 in the OT) — Egyptian embalming involved removing internal organs, treating the body with natron (a salt compound), and wrapping in linen. Rōpĕʾîm (physicians, H7495, from rāpāʾ, to heal) — Joseph employs his personal physicians for the task. The use of Egyptian mummification for a Hebrew patriarch signals Jacob's honorary Egyptian status.
3: wayyimĕlĕʾû-lô ʾarbaʿîm yôm kî kēn yimĕlĕʾû yĕmê haḥănuṭîm wayyibkû ʾōtô miṣrāyim šibʿîm yôm — "It took a full forty days, for that was the time required for embalming. And the Egyptians mourned him seventy days." Two time-periods: 40 days for the embalming process (natron desiccation took approximately 40 days according to Egyptian records) + 70 days of mourning. Herodotus records that mourning for an Egyptian pharaoh was 72 days; Diodorus Siculus confirms 70 days. Jacob receives a royal Egyptian mourning period. Wayyibkû ʾōtô miṣrāyim (the Egyptians mourned him) — pagan Egypt mourns the Hebrew patriarch; the covenant-people's blessing on surrounding nations (Genesis 12:3) is visible in the nation's grief.
4: ʾim-nāʾ māṣāʾtî ḥēn bĕʿênêḵem — "If I have found favor (māṣāʾtî ḥēn) in your eyes." Joseph speaks to Pharaoh's household rather than directly to Pharaoh — possibly because he is in a state of ritual mourning-uncleanness (contact with the dead made one impure in both Egyptian and Israelite law), which would prevent direct access to the royal court. The māṣāʾ ḥēn (found grace/favor) formula — the covenant-favor language used throughout the patriarchal narrative (Genesis 6:8; Genesis 32:5; Genesis 39:4) — is here used diplomatically: Joseph frames his burial request as a petition of grace rather than a demand of position.
5: bĕqiḇrî ʾăšer kārîtî-llî bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan šāmmāh tiqqāḇĕrēnî — "in the tomb I dug (kārîtî) for myself in the land of Canaan, there bury me." Kārāh (H3738, to dig/prepare/purchase) — the verb for digging a grave or a well; the same word used for digging cisterns (Numbers 21:18; 2 Chronicles 16:14: Asa's rock-cut tomb). Jacob prepared his grave in advance — the legal precision of his burial request includes self-preparation of the grave site. The Machpelah cave was both legally purchased (Genesis 23) and personally prepared by Jacob.
6: wayyōʾmer parʿōh ʿălēh ûqĕḇōr ʾet-ʾāḇîḵā kaʾăšer hišbîʿăḵā — "Pharaoh said, 'Go and bury your father, as he made you swear.'" Pharaoh's one-sentence authorization honors the oath-obligation — hišbîʿăḵā (he made you swear, Hiphil of šāḇaʿ, H7650) acknowledges that oath-keeping supersedes even Egyptian administrative convenience. The honor given to an oath-obligation from a foreign patriarch by the Egyptian king is a narrative comment on the covenant-weight of Israel's promise-system.
7: wayyaʿal yôsēp liqbōr ʾet-ʾāḇîw wayyaʿălû ʾittô kāl-ʿaḇdê parʿōh ziqnê bêtô wĕḵōl ziqnê ʾereṣ-miṣrāyim — "Joseph went up to bury his father. All Pharaoh's officials went up with him — the dignitaries of his household and all the dignitaries of Egypt." The funeral procession is a state affair: ʿaḇdê parʿōh (Pharaoh's officials) + ziqnê bêtô (elders of his household) + ziqnê ʾereṣ-miṣrāyim (elders of all Egypt). Zāqēn (H2205, elder — the senior official) — the triple set of Egyptian dignitaries constitutes a diplomatic and military honor-guard for the patriarch's burial.
8: ûḇêt yôsēp wĕʾeḥāyw ûḇêt ʾāḇîw raq ṭappām wĕṣōʾnām ûḇĕqārām ʿāzĕḇû bĕʾereṣ gōšen — "the entire household of Joseph, his brothers, and his father's household. Only their children and their flocks and herds were left in Goshen." The men go on the burial journey; the children, flocks, and herds remain in Goshen as the living covenant-community awaiting return. This mirrors the later Exodus pattern: Israel's livestock will be a point of negotiation with Pharaoh (Exodus 10:24-26). The little ones and animals left behind are the security-deposit for Israel's return to Egypt.
9: wayyaʿal ʿimmô gam-rekeb gam-pārāšîm wayhî hammahăneh kāḇēḏ mĕʾōḏ — "Chariots and horsemen also went up with him. It was a very large company." Rekeb (H7393, chariot/chariotry) + pārāšîm (H6571, horsemen/cavalry) — military escort for the state funeral procession. Hammahăneh kāḇēḏ mĕʾōḏ (a very heavy/large company) — kāḇēḏ (heavy, the word for the famine's severity in Genesis 43:1) here means "impressive/large in scale." A heavy procession is an honorable procession.
10: wayyābōʾû ʿaḏ-gōren hāʾāṭāḏ ʾăšer bĕʿēḇer hayyardēn wayyispĕḏû-šām mispēḏ gāḏôl wĕḵāḇēḏ mĕʾōḏ — "When they reached the threshing floor of Atad, near the Jordan, they mourned loudly and bitterly." Gōren hāʾāṭāḏ (threshing floor of the bramble/thorn-bush) — the location's name is evocative; thorns and mourning belong together. The mourning produces a new place-name: ʾāḇēl miṣrāyim (mourning of Egypt). ʾĀḇēl carries a double resonance: the common noun for mourning and the personal name Abel — the first death in human history names the place of the covenant-patriarch's mourning.
11: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾēḇel-kāḇēḏ zeh lĕmiṣrāyim ʿal-kēn qārāʾ šĕmāh ʾāḇēl miṣrāyim — "They said, 'This is a solemn mourning for the Egyptians' — hence the name Abel Mizraim." ʾĀḇēl miṣrāyim (Mourning of Egypt) — the etiology explains the place-name from the Canaanite perspective: they see Egyptian mourning and name the place for it. The Canaanites, who will later be dispossessed by Israel, inadvertently name a site on the Jordan's east bank for Egypt's grief over a Hebrew patriarch.
12: wayyaʿăśû ḇānāyw lô kēn kaʾăšer ṣiwwām — "Jacob's sons did for him exactly as he commanded them." Kaʾăšer ṣiwwām (just as he commanded them) — the command-fulfillment formula confirms that Jacob's deathbed instructions have been carried out to the letter. The same formula governs the tabernacle construction (Exodus 39:43: "Moses saw that they had done it just as YHWH commanded") — obedient execution of a revered authority's instructions.
13: wayyiśśĕʾû ʾōtô bānāyw ʾarṣāh kĕnaʿan wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô bimĕʿārat śĕḏêh hammaḵpēlāh — "His sons carried him to the land of Canaan and buried him in the cave in the field of Machpelah." The burial completes the covenant-geography: Jacob's body returns to Canaan even while his sons remain in Egypt. His burial is a covenant-advance-payment — the promised land receives the patriarch's bones before the nation that will inhabit that land has formed. Hebrews 11:22 connects this pattern to faith: Joseph's instructions about his own bones (v25) are listed as an act of faith, looking forward to the Exodus.
14: wayyāšāḇ yôsēp miṣrāymāh hûʾ wĕʾeḥāyw wĕḵol-hāʿōlîm ʾittô liqbōr ʾet-ʾāḇîw — "After burying his father, Joseph returned to Egypt — he, his brothers, and all who had gone up with him." The return to Egypt resets the narrative frame: the brothers who momentarily stood in the promised land are back in Egypt. The phrase "all who had gone up" (hāʿōlîm, from ʿālāh, to go up) — the ascending movement to Canaan and the descending return to Egypt trace the pattern Israel will follow until the Exodus.
15: wayyirʾû ʾăḥê yôsēp kî-mēt ʾăḇîhem wayyōʾmĕrû lû yiśṭĕmēnû yôsēp — "When Joseph's brothers saw that their father was dead, they said, 'What if Joseph holds a grudge (yiśṭĕmēnû) against us?'" Śāṭam (H7852, to bear a grudge/persecute/nurse enmity — a rare verb) — the same word used for the archers' hostility toward Joseph in Genesis 49:23 (wayyiśṭĕmûhû). The brothers fear that Joseph's restraint during Jacob's lifetime was diplomatic — now that Jacob is gone, they fear the grudge-keeping that their own culture would practice. The fear is psychologically realistic: honor-shame cultures maintain obligations of vengeance that are deferred for social reasons and executed when the constraint dissolves.
16: wayyĕṣawwû ʾel-yôsēp lēʾmōr ʾāḇîḵā ṣiwwāh liṗnê môtô lēʾmōr — "So they sent word to Joseph, saying, 'Your father gave instructions before he died.'" The narrative does not record Jacob giving this instruction — it appears only here in the brothers' message. Whether Jacob actually gave this deathbed instruction or the brothers fabricated it is deliberately left ambiguous by the narrator. The message invokes Jacob's authority (ṣiwwāh, commanded) to make an appeal that Jacob is no longer alive to verify. The rhetorical use of a patriarch's posthumous authority is attested in ANE family law; the brothers may be using a legitimate legal form regardless of historicity.
17: śĕʾû-nāʾ pešaʿ ʾaḥêḵā wĕḥaṭṭāʾtām kî-rāʿāh gĕmālûḵā wayyēḇk yôsēp bĕḏabbĕrām ʾēlāyw — "Please forgive the transgression (pešaʿ) of your brothers and their sin (ḥaṭṭāʾtām), for they did evil against you." Two words for offense: pešaʿ (H6588, transgression/rebellion — the most serious category of sin, deliberate violation of a relationship) + ḥaṭṭāʾāh (H2403, sin/offense — the general term). Wayyēḇk yôsēp (and Joseph wept) — the weeping response confirms the authenticity of his earlier forgiveness (Genesis 45:1-15): the brothers' fear and appeal move him to tears, not to vindictiveness.
18: wayyēlĕḵû gam-ʾeḥāyw wayyippĕlû lĕpānāyw wayyōʾmĕrû hinnennû lĕḵā laʿăḇāḏîm — "His brothers then came and fell down before him and said, 'We are your slaves.'" Wayyippĕlû lĕpānāyw (they fell before his face) — the final fulfillment of Joseph's second dream (Genesis 37:9-10: "the sun and moon and eleven stars bowed to me"). In 43:26 they bowed; in 44:14 they fell; now in 50:18 they fall before him and verbally confirm total submission. The phrase hinnennû lĕḵā laʿăḇāḏîm (behold, we are your slaves) — the maximum self-abnegation in ANE social vocabulary. Joseph's response will refuse this diminishment.
19: wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem yôsēp ʾal-tîrāʾû kî hătaḥat ʾĕlōhîm ʾānî — "Joseph said to them, 'Don't be afraid. Am I in the place (taḥat) of God?'" Hătaḥat ʾĕlōhîm ʾānî (am I in place of God?) — taḥat (H8478, under/in place of) — the same preposition used for Judah's substitution offer (Genesis 44:33: taḥat hannáʿar, in place of the boy) and the ram in Isaac's place (Genesis 22:13: taḥat bĕnô, in place of his son). Joseph uses the substitution-preposition to ask a theological question: revenge is the divine prerogative, not his. By refusing to occupy the place of God, he creates the space for grace. The rhetorical question expects "no" — Joseph is not God; therefore he will not execute divine vengeance.
20: wĕʾattem ḥăšaḇtem ʿālay rāʿāh ʾĕlōhîm ḥăšāḇāh lĕṭōḇāh — "You intended (ḥăšaḇtem) evil against me, but God intended (ḥăšāḇāh) it for good." The theological pivot of the entire narrative: ḥāšaḇ (H2803, to think/plan/intend/account/reckon/purpose) appears twice — once with the brothers as subject (evil intent) and once with God as subject (good intent). The same event is the object of two simultaneous purposes: human wickedness and divine redemption. This is the Genesis statement of concursive divine sovereignty — the full reality of both intentions without collapsing either into the other. Acts 2:23 reproduces this structure for the crucifixion: "delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God… you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men." Lĕmaʿan ʿăśôh kǎyyôm hazzeh lĕhaḥăyôt ʿam-rāḇ — lĕhaḥăyôt (Hiphil of ḥāyāh: to cause to live/preserve life) — the life-preservation purpose articulated at the revelation (Genesis 45:5-7) is now the capstone of Joseph's theological statement.
21: wĕʿattāh ʾal-tîrāʾû ʾānōḵî ʾăḵalkēl ʾetḵem wĕʾet-ṭapĕḵem — "So don't be afraid. I will provide for (ʾăḵalkēl) you and your children." ʾĂḵalkēl (Pilpel of kûl, H3557, to sustain/provide for/maintain) — the intensive form of the provision-verb; the same word used for Joseph's promise in Genesis 45:11 (wĕḵilkaltî ʾōtĕḵā). Joseph's comprehensive care extends to the next generation (ṭapĕḵem, your little ones). Waynnaḥēm ʾōtām wayyĕḏabbēr ʿal-libbām (he comforted them and spoke to their hearts) — the idiom dāḇar ʿal-lēḇ (to speak to the heart) is the language of deep intimate consolation (Genesis 34:3; Ruth 2:13; Isaiah 40:2: "speak tenderly to Jerusalem").
22: wayyēšeḇ yôsēp bĕmiṣrāyim hûʾ ûḇêt ʾāḇîw wayyĕḥî yôsēp mĕʾāh wĕʿeśer šānîm — "Joseph stayed in Egypt, along with all his father's family. He lived a hundred and ten years." Mĕʾāh wĕʿeśer šānîm (a hundred and ten years) — 110 years was the ideal lifespan in Egyptian culture: the Maxims of Ptahhotep (ca. 2400 BCE) states that 110 years is the ideal. Joseph dies at exactly the Egyptian ideal age — honored by the culture that received him, dying with the covenant-promise still outstanding, his bones still in Egypt awaiting the Exodus.
23: wayyarʾ yôsēp lĕʾeprayim bĕnê šillēšîm gam bĕnê māḵîr ben-mĕnaššeh yullĕḏû ʿal-birkê yôsēp — "Joseph saw the third generation of Ephraim's children; also the children of Makir son of Manasseh were born on Joseph's knees." Two generation-markers: Ephraim's third generation (great-grandchildren) and Manasseh's grandchildren (bĕnê māḵîr). Yullĕḏû ʿal-birkê yôsēp (born on Joseph's knees) — the adoption-formula: receiving a child "on the knees" constituted formal adoption into the family (cf. Jacob's adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh, Genesis 48:12). Joseph officially adopts Makir's children, extending his covenant-line an additional generation.
24: wayyōʾmer yôsēp ʾel-ʾeḥāyw ʾānōḵî mēt wēʾlōhîm pāqōḏ yipqōḏ ʾetḵem — "Joseph said to his brothers, 'I am about to die. But God will surely visit (pāqōḏ yipqōḏ) you.'" Pāqōḏ yipqōḏ (Qal infinitive absolute + imperfect of pāqaḏ, H6485) — the emphatic covenant-attendance formula: "visiting he will visit" = "God will certainly attend to you." Pāqaḏ is the covenant-visitation verb: God "visited" Sarah (Genesis 21:1: waYHWH pāqaḏ ʾet-śārāh). The specific formula pāqōḏ yipqōḏ will be the sign-word for the Exodus: Exodus 3:16 (God to Moses: "I have surely visited [pāqōḏ pāqaḏtî] you") and Exodus 13:19 (Moses carries Joseph's bones because Joseph used this exact formula). Joseph's dying word is the Exodus-promise.
25: haʿălōh taʿălû ʾet-ʿaṣmōtay mizzeh — "You shall certainly carry my bones (ʿaṣmōtay) up from here." Haʿălōh taʿălû (Hiphil infinitive absolute + imperfect: "you will certainly bring up") — the emphatic oath-formula for absolute certainty. ʿAṣmōtay (my bones, H6106) — bones in the OT are the seat of identity, strength, and covenant-continuity; carrying Joseph's bones to Canaan carries Joseph himself to the promise. The oath is fulfilled in Exodus 13:19 (Moses carries the bones at the Exodus) and Joshua 24:32 (burial at Shechem). Hebrews 11:22 explicitly cites this bone-instruction as an act of faith: "by faith, Joseph, when dying, made mention of the departure of the Israelites and gave directions about his bones." The bones are the material covenant-sign that Joseph belongs to the promise, not to Egypt.
26: wayyāmot yôsēp ben-mĕʾāh wĕʿeśer šānîm wayyaḥănĕṭû ʾōtô wayyîšem bāʾārôn bĕmiṣrāyim — "And Joseph died at the age of a hundred and ten. After they embalmed him, he was placed in a coffin (ʾārôn) in Egypt." The closing word of Genesis is bĕmiṣrāyim — "in Egypt." The book that began with creation and the garden ends with a coffin in Egypt — the covenant-people's sojourn in the land from which they will be delivered. ʾĀrôn (H727, chest/coffin/box) — this is the identical word used for the Ark of the Covenant (ʾărôn habbĕrît). Joseph's coffin-ʾārôn and the Ark-ʾārôn share the same Hebrew word: the covenant-promise travels in a chest through the wilderness. The last word of Genesis points forward to Exodus — the coffin awaiting the departure, the bones awaiting the promise.
